text: NHRA (National Hot Rod Association) is the primary sanctioning body for drag racing in North America, founded by Wally Parks in 1951. It sanctions over 20 national events per year across four professional categories: Top Fuel, Funny Car, Pro Stock, and Pro Stock Motorcycle.

A standard drag strip is 1,320 feet (quarter mile). Top Fuel dragsters cover this distance in under 3.7 seconds at over 330 mph. The acceleration exceeds 5 G-forces, and drivers experience temporary vision loss from blood draining away from their eyes.

Top Fuel dragsters produce approximately 11,000 horsepower from a 500 cubic inch supercharged Hemi engine burning nitromethane. They consume approximately 22 gallons of fuel per run (about 1.2 gallons per second). The engine is completely rebuilt after every pass.

The Christmas Tree is the starting light system in drag racing. It consists of staging lights (amber), three countdown amber bulbs (or one in Pro Tree), a green 'go' light, and a red foul light. A 'perfect light' or 'perfect reaction time' is .000 seconds. Pro Tree uses a single amber that goes directly to green after 0.400 seconds.

Elapsed Time (ET) measures the time from the green light to crossing the finish line. Trap speed is the vehicle's speed measured in the last 66 feet before the finish. In bracket racing, the goal is consistency to your dial-in, not outright speed — going faster than your dial-in is a breakout and means you lose.

Funny Cars are similar to Top Fuel dragsters but have a shorter wheelbase and a one-piece flip-up carbon fiber body that resembles a production car. They also run nitromethane and produce similar horsepower but are slightly slower due to the aerodynamic penalty of the body. Current records are around 3.8 seconds at 330+ mph.

Pro Stock is the 'factory hot rod' class of NHRA. Cars must maintain stock appearance and use naturally aspirated engines (no superchargers, turbos, or nitrous). They run low 6-second quarter miles at over 210 mph. Engine displacement is limited to 500 cubic inches. The class emphasizes engine building precision and tuning over raw forced-induction power.

Nitromethane (CH3NO2) is the fuel used in Top Fuel and Funny Car classes. It carries its own oxygen, allowing engines to burn much more fuel per cycle than gasoline. The combustion is so violent that spark plugs only fire to start the engine — after that, combustion is sustained by heat and compression alone.

Street racing evolved separately from organized drag racing and typically involves racing on public roads, often from a standing start or a roll. The culture was documented in films like 'The Fast and the Furious' (2001) but has deep roots in 1950s American hot rod culture, particularly in Southern California.

Don 'Big Daddy' Garlits is considered the father of Top Fuel drag racing. He pioneered the rear-engine dragster design in 1971 after a transmission explosion severed part of his foot. Moving the engine behind the driver made dragsters dramatically safer. He was the first to break 170, 180, 200, 250, and 270 mph in the quarter mile.

Shirley Muldowney was the first woman licensed by NHRA to drive a Top Fuel dragster (1974) and the first woman to win an NHRA national event in Top Fuel. She won three NHRA Top Fuel championships (1977, 1980, 1982). Her story was told in the 1983 film 'Heart Like a Wheel.'

John Force is the winningest driver in NHRA history with 16 Funny Car championships and over 155 national event wins. He began racing in 1978 and was still competing into his 70s. His team, John Force Racing, has been a dynasty in Funny Car for four decades.

A burnout before a drag race serves two purposes: it heats the rear tires to increase grip (hot rubber is stickier) and it lays down a layer of rubber on the track surface in the driver's lane. The burnout box has water (or bleach in some classes) to help the tires spin and heat up. Burnouts can last 5-15 seconds depending on the class.

Bracket racing is the most common form of amateur drag racing. Drivers 'dial in' their predicted ET. The slower car gets a head start calculated from the difference in dial-ins. The first to cross the finish line wins, UNLESS they go faster than their dial-in (breakout), in which case they lose. This makes it a game of consistency, not speed.

The 'no-prep' racing movement, popularized by TV shows like Street Outlaws, involves drag racing on unprepped surfaces (no traction compound applied to the track). This simulates street racing conditions where traction is unpredictable. Cars must manage tire spin, which makes driving skill as important as horsepower.

Nitrous oxide (N2O, 'NOS') systems inject nitrous into the engine intake, which decomposes at high temperature to release additional oxygen, allowing more fuel to be burned. A typical street nitrous kit adds 75-300 horsepower. 'Wet' systems inject fuel with the nitrous; 'dry' systems add only nitrous and rely on the fuel system to compensate.

A supercharger (blower) is a belt-driven compressor that forces more air into the engine. In drag racing, Roots-type blowers (the big rectangular boxes on top of the engine) are iconic. A typical drag racing blower produces 30+ PSI of boost. The 14-71 blower is the most common size in professional drag racing, named after its original application in a Detroit Diesel 14-cylinder, 71 cubic inch per cylinder engine.

Turbochargers use exhaust gases to spin a turbine that compresses intake air. In drag racing, large single turbos or twin turbo setups can produce enormous power — Pro Mod turbo cars make over 4,000 horsepower. The lag inherent in turbo systems (spool time) is managed through anti-lag systems, high-stall torque converters, or transbrake launches.

A transbrake simultaneously applies the forward and reverse gear in an automatic transmission, holding the car stationary while the engine builds torque against the converter. When released, the stored energy launches the car explosively. Transbrake launches are standard in bracket and heads-up racing and can improve 60-foot times by several tenths.

The 60-foot time is the most critical measurement in drag racing — it measures how quickly the car covers the first 60 feet from the starting line. A good 60-foot time indicates the car launched well with good traction. In Top Fuel, the 60-foot time is under 0.85 seconds. In a street car running 12-second quarters, a 1.8-second 60-foot is considered good.

Wheelie bars are long bars extending behind the rear axle of drag cars to prevent the front end from lifting too high during launch. Without them, high-powered rear-wheel-drive cars can flip backward from torque reaction. They're mandatory in many racing classes and adjustable — lower settings allow less wheelstand.

Slicks are smooth, treadless tires used in drag racing for maximum contact patch and grip. They are made of soft rubber compounds that become sticky when heated. Wrinkle-wall slicks have thin sidewalls that visibly deform during launch, acting as a mechanical shock absorber that helps plant the tire and improve traction.

Pro Mod (Pro Modified) is one of the most diverse and exciting NHRA classes. Cars can use superchargers, turbos, or nitrous — any power adder. Bodies must resemble production vehicles from 1941 or newer. Quarter-mile times are in the 5.6-5.8 second range at over 250 mph. The variety of power combinations makes it unpredictable.

The Hemi engine (named for its hemispherical combustion chambers) is the most iconic engine in drag racing history. Chrysler's 426 Hemi, introduced in 1964, dominated NASCAR and drag racing so thoroughly it was banned from NASCAR. Modern NHRA Top Fuel and Funny Car engines are based on the Hemi design, bored and stroked to 500 cubic inches.

Roll racing (also called roll-on racing) starts from a rolling speed (typically 30-60 mph) rather than a standing start. It eliminates the launch as a variable and tests pure power-to-speed capability. Popular in modern street racing culture, especially with turbocharged imports where builds make enormous top-end power but struggle with traction from a dig.

The Chevrolet small block (SBC), introduced in 1955, is the most popular engine in drag racing history for amateur and sportsman classes. Its compact size, light weight, extensive aftermarket support, and reliable architecture make it the default choice for budget drag builds. The LS-series (Gen III/IV small block) has largely replaced it in modern builds.

Pomona Raceway (now called In-N-Out Burger Pomona Dragstrip at Fairplex) in Pomona, California, hosts the NHRA Winternationals (season opener) and the NHRA Finals (season closer). It's considered the most prestigious venue in drag racing. The track opened in 1961 and has been the site of countless records and championship-deciding moments.

Street Outlaws, the Discovery Channel reality series that began in 2013, popularized no-prep and street racing culture to mainstream audiences. Based in Oklahoma City, it features real street racers competing for rankings on 'The List.' The show spawned multiple spinoffs and helped legitimize no-prep events at sanctioned facilities.

A powerglide is a two-speed automatic transmission favored in drag racing for its simplicity, light weight (about 75 lbs), and ability to handle enormous power. With only two forward gears, it has minimal parasitic loss and shifts quickly. Most bracket racers and many Pro Mod cars use powerglides. The aftermarket offers billet cases rated for 3,000+ horsepower.

Data acquisition systems in modern drag racing record dozens of parameters per run: engine RPM, boost pressure, exhaust gas temperature (EGT), driveshaft speed, clutch slip, suspension travel, and more. Tuners analyze this data between rounds to optimize fuel maps, ignition timing, and clutch management. In Top Fuel, the crew chief's ability to read data and adjust for changing conditions is often the difference between winning and losing.

Weight transfer during a drag launch shifts load from the front tires to the rear tires, which is why drag cars squat in the rear and lift the front. Proper suspension setup (ladder bars, four-links, or traction bars) controls how this weight transfers. Too aggressive = tire spin; too soft = slow 60-foot. The art of drag racing suspension tuning is matching weight transfer to available traction.

Import drag racing gained mainstream attention in the late 1990s with Hondas, DSMs (Eclipse/Talon), and Supras running deep into the 9s and 8s. The 2JZ-GTE (Toyota Supra) engine became legendary for its ability to handle 1,500+ horsepower on stock internals. The 4G63 (Mitsubishi) and K-series (Honda) followed. Import drag racing proved that displacement isn't everything — boost is.

JDM (Japanese Domestic Market) legends in drag racing include the Toyota Supra (2JZ), Nissan GT-R (RB26), Mitsubishi Evo (4G63), Honda Civic/Integra (B/K-series), and Mazda RX-7 (13B rotary). These platforms proved that forced induction on lightweight cars could compete with and beat traditional American V8 muscle. The rivalry between import and domestic drag racers defined the 2000s.

Roadkill, the MotorTrend show starring David Freiburger and Mike Finnegan, embodies the junkyard hot rod spirit. They build cars from forgotten wrecks, drive them cross-country, and inevitably break down repeatedly. Their drag racing episodes feature barely-running heaps making surprisingly fast passes. The show's appeal is authenticity — real builds, real failures, real friendship.

Engine Masters, hosted by Steve Dulcich and David Freiburger, is a dyno-testing show where engine configurations are tested head-to-head. Episodes settle real-world debates: headers vs manifolds, carb vs EFI, single-plane vs dual-plane intake, cam duration effects. Every test is measured on a Superflow 902 engine dyno with repeatable methodology.

The 'Holeshot' in drag racing occurs when the slower car wins by having a better reaction time at the tree. Even if Car B runs a faster ET, Car A can win if they left the line significantly earlier. In bracket racing, holeshots are common and strategic. A .010 reaction time advantage means the other driver must make up that gap with speed.

Parachutes are mandatory safety equipment on cars running faster than 150 mph in the quarter mile (NHRA rules). Top Fuel and Funny Car deploy dual chutes at the end of each run, experiencing deceleration forces of 4-5 G. At 330+ mph, the car needs the entire shutdown area (typically 2,500+ feet) to stop safely even with parachutes.

The scatter shield (or blowproof bellhousing) is a steel or ballistic nylon enclosure around the flywheel and clutch area, mandatory in NHRA for cars running faster than certain thresholds. Clutch and flywheel explosions are one of the most dangerous failures in drag racing — fragments can exit the car at bullet speeds. SFI certification ensures shields meet impact resistance standards.

A two-step is an electronic rev limiter with two RPM settings: a lower limit for launch (controlled by a button on the brake or transbrake) and the normal higher limit. The lower limit builds boost in turbocharged cars while staged, and creates the signature flame-spitting anti-lag effect. When the driver releases the button, the engine jumps to full RPM for launch.

Methanol (wood alcohol, CH3OH) is used as fuel in some drag racing classes, particularly alcohol dragsters and funny cars. It burns cooler than gasoline, allowing higher compression ratios, and produces about 20% less energy per gallon — but engines can burn more of it, netting a power gain. It's also safer: methanol fires are nearly invisible but can be extinguished with water.

The Pro Tree (used in professional NHRA classes) flashes all three amber lights simultaneously, then green after 0.400 seconds. The sportsman tree (used in bracket racing) cascades the three ambers sequentially at 0.500-second intervals. The pro tree requires faster reaction times and is more dramatic to watch.

Dry lake racing at El Mirage and Muroc (now Edwards AFB) in Southern California during the 1930s-40s was the direct precursor to organized drag racing. Hot rodders raced on flat lake beds for top speed, establishing the car culture that would move to dedicated strips after WWII.

The first organized drag race is generally credited to the Santa Ana Drags, held on a former military airstrip in Santa Ana, California, starting in 1950. C.J. 'Pappy' Hart organized the events to get hot rodders off public streets.

Wally Parks founded the National Hot Rod Association (NHRA) in 1951 to promote safety and organization in hot rod racing. He was editor of Hot Rod Magazine and leveraged the publication to build NHRA membership nationwide.
The first NHRA Nationals were held at Great Bend, Kansas, in 1955. Calvin Rice won Top Eliminator with a run of 134.21 mph in a Chrysler-powered dragster.

Lions Drag Strip in Long Beach, California (1955-1972) was one of the most famous drag strips in history. It hosted the first NHRA Winternationals in 1961 before the event moved to Pomona. The strip closed when the land was reclaimed for port expansion.

The Chrysler 354 Hemi, introduced in 1951, was the first overhead-valve V8 to dominate drag racing. Its hemispherical combustion chambers allowed larger valves and better breathing than flathead or wedge designs, establishing Chrysler's dominance in drag racing that continues today.

Slingshot dragsters, named for the driver sitting behind the rear axle, were the dominant Top Fuel design from the late 1950s through 1970. The long wheelbase aided traction but exposed the driver to engine and transmission failures. Don Garlits lost part of his foot to a transmission explosion in a slingshot in 1970.

Don Garlits ran the first official 200 mph quarter mile pass in 1964 at Great Meadows, New Jersey, with a speed of 201.34 mph. He went on to break virtually every significant speed barrier in Top Fuel through the 1980s.

The first 7-second quarter mile pass in Top Fuel was achieved by Don Garlits in 1975 with a 7.62 at Gainesville, Florida. Each second barrier in Top Fuel represented a massive leap in engine technology, tire development, and chassis engineering.

Funny Cars evolved from 'factory experimental' (FX) altered-wheelbase stock cars in the mid-1960s. Manufacturers moved the front wheels forward for better weight transfer, creating cars that looked 'funny' compared to stock. The one-piece flip-up fiberglass body appeared by 1966.

Sox & Martin (Ronnie Sox and Buddy Martin) dominated Pro Stock in its early years, winning the first NHRA Pro Stock championship in 1970. Ronnie Sox was famous for his four-speed manual shifting — he could shift faster than most automatic transmissions.

Jungle Jim Liberman was one of drag racing's first showmen in the 1960s-70s, known for spectacular burnouts, wild driving, and his partnership with 'Jungle Pam' Hardy. He died in a street accident in 1977 at age 31, and is still considered one of the most charismatic figures in drag racing history.

Bill 'Grumpy' Jenkins was the godfather of Pro Stock, dominating the class in the early 1970s with Chevy Camaros and Vegas. His engine building skills were legendary — he was the first Pro Stock racer to use computer-aided engine development.

TV Tommy Ivo was a child actor turned drag racer who built the first four-engine dragster in 1961 — four Buick nailhead engines mounted in a single chassis. He later ran a twin-engine Funny Car. His showmanship and Hollywood connections helped popularize drag racing to mainstream audiences.

The Ramchargers were a team of Chrysler engineers who drag raced on weekends, building some of the fastest cars in NHRA history during the 1960s. They developed innovations that found their way into production Chrysler vehicles, including improved intake manifold designs and high-performance camshaft profiles.

The Greer-Black-Prudhomme dragster, driven by Don Prudhomme in the mid-1960s, was one of the most successful Top Fuel cars ever built. It set multiple records and established Prudhomme (later known as 'The Snake') as a rising star.

Don Garlits pioneered the rear-engine Top Fuel dragster in 1971 after a catastrophic transmission explosion in his front-engine slingshot severed part of his foot at Lions Drag Strip. His 'Swamp Rat XIV' moved the engine behind the driver, and within two years every competitive Top Fuel team copied the design.

The Snake vs Mongoose rivalry between Don Prudhomme and Tom McEwen defined 1970s drag racing. Their Mattel Hot Wheels sponsorship deal (1970) was the first major corporate sponsorship in drag racing, and the toy cars introduced millions of children to the sport.

The 1973 oil crisis devastated drag racing. Many tracks closed, and the NHRA shortened the season. Nitromethane prices skyrocketed, and several Top Fuel teams switched to alcohol or shut down entirely. The sport didn't fully recover until the late 1970s.

Pro Stock was created by NHRA in 1970 to give factory-appearing stock cars their own professional class. The original rules required production-based engines with no superchargers or nitrous. Bob Glidden became the most successful Pro Stock driver ever with 85 national event wins and 10 championships.

Kenny Bernstein became the first driver to exceed 300 mph in NHRA competition on March 20, 1992, at Gainesville Raceway in his Budweiser King Top Fuel dragster with a speed of 301.70 mph. The milestone had been anticipated for years and was a massive media event.
Joe Amato was the first Top Fuel driver to break the 280 mph barrier (1984) and 260 mph barrier (1984), pioneering the 4-second era. He won five NHRA Top Fuel championships between 1984 and 1996.

Bob Glidden won 85 NHRA national events and 10 Pro Stock championships between 1973 and 1995, making him the most dominant driver in Pro Stock history. He was famous for meticulous engine preparation and a fiercely competitive personality.
Eddie Hill was the first drag racer to break the 4.99-second barrier in Top Fuel at the 1988 Texas Motorplex, running 4.990 seconds. He was also a champion boat racer before switching to dragsters.

The import drag racing revolution began in the early 1990s when turbocharged Hondas, Mitsubishi Eclipses (DSMs), and Toyota Supras started running 10-second quarter miles — previously thought impossible for four-cylinder engines. Jun Bonnhoff's 8-second Supra in 1999 stunned the drag racing world.

Tony Schumacher won eight NHRA Top Fuel championships (1999-2000, 2004-2009, 2014), earning the nickname 'The Sarge' for his Army sponsorship. His father, Don Schumacher, built a multi-car racing empire that became the most successful team owner operation in NHRA history.

The Don Schumacher Racing (DSR) organization fielded up to six professional NHRA cars simultaneously, dominating both Top Fuel and Funny Car for over two decades. At its peak, DSR cars won more than half of all professional NHRA events in a season.

Ashley Force Hood became the first woman to win an NHRA Funny Car national event in 2008 at the Pomona Winternationals, driving for her father John Force's team. She defeated her father in the final round.

The NHRA Countdown to the Championship (now called the NHRA Countdown to the Championship Playoffs) was introduced in 2007, resetting the top 10 drivers' points after the first 18 events for a six-race playoff format. It was modeled after NASCAR's Chase format to create more excitement in the final races.

Street Outlaws premiered on Discovery Channel on June 10, 2013, featuring Oklahoma City street racers competing for rankings on 'The List.' Big Chief, Murder Nova (Shawn Ellington), Farmtruck & AZN, and Daddy Dave became household names. The show legitimized street racing culture and spawned a media empire.

Ryan Martin emerged as the dominant figure in no-prep racing, winning multiple Street Outlaws: No Prep Kings championships with his twin-turbo Fireball Camaro. His consistent success in the unpredictable no-prep format established him as one of the most skilled racers in the modern era.

Lizzy Musi, daughter of legendary engine builder Pat Musi, was one of the fastest female drag racers in history, competing in Pro Nitrous and Radial vs the World classes. She ran 3.6-second eighth-mile passes. She tragically passed away in 2024 from breast cancer at age 25.

Scott Kalitta, son of drag racing legend Connie Kalitta, died on June 21, 2008, when his Top Fuel dragster failed to stop at Old Bridge Township Raceway Park. His death led to the NHRA shortening the Top Fuel and Funny Car distance from 1,320 feet (quarter mile) to 1,000 feet, a change that remains in effect.

Eric Medlen, a rising star Funny Car driver for John Force Racing, died on March 23, 2007, from injuries sustained during a testing crash at Gainesville. He was 33. His death, combined with Kalitta's the following year, fundamentally changed NHRA safety standards.

Brittany Force, John Force's daughter, set the Top Fuel elapsed time record of 3.623 seconds at the 2019 NHRA Winternationals at Pomona. She won the 2017 Top Fuel championship, becoming the second Force family member to win a professional title.

The Tesla Model S Plaid ran a 9.23-second quarter mile at 152.2 mph in stock form, making it the fastest production car ever in the quarter mile when released in 2021. It demonstrated that electric vehicles could compete with purpose-built drag cars.

The Rimac Nevera holds the electric car quarter-mile record at 8.582 seconds at 167.51 mph, set in 2023. With 1,914 horsepower from four electric motors and instant torque, it demonstrates the future potential of EV drag racing.

Radial vs the World (RVW) emerged in the 2010s as one of the most exciting drag racing classes. Cars must run DOT-approved radial tires (not slicks) but have virtually no other restrictions on power. Runs in the 3.5-second range at 210+ mph on radials are now common, representing an engineering challenge that pure slick-tire racing doesn't have.

Drag Week, organized by Hot Rod Magazine, is an annual event where competitors must drive their drag car on public streets between five different drag strips over five days, running consistent ETs at each. Tom Bailey's Camaro dominated the event, running 5-second quarter miles and then driving 1,000+ miles on the highway.

Cleetus McFarland (Garrett Mitchell) became one of the most popular automotive YouTubers by building drag cars including 'Leroy' (twin-turbo C5 Corvette) and 'Ruby' (Holley EFI turbo Corvette). He purchased and renovated Bradenton Motorsports Park in Florida, renamed it 'Freedom Factory,' creating a grassroots racing destination.

1320video (now called 1320) is a YouTube channel that became the definitive documenter of grassroots street and drag racing, capturing events at tracks nationwide and filming street races. Their coverage of small-tire racing, roll racing, and no-prep events helped build the modern grassroots drag racing community.

The small block Chevy LS engine (Gen III/IV) revolutionized drag racing in the 2000s-2010s. Its aluminum block, cathedral or rectangular port heads, and enormous aftermarket support made it the default choice for everything from budget bracket racers to 3,000+ horsepower Pro Mod cars. The LSX bowtie block can handle over 3,500 horsepower.

The Toyota 2JZ-GTE engine from the MK4 Supra (1993-2002) became the most iconic import drag racing engine. Its cast-iron block with forged crankshaft handled 1,500+ horsepower on stock internals. Builds with aftermarket single turbos routinely run 7-second quarter miles.

The Mitsubishi 4G63 engine, used in the Eclipse GSX and Evolution series, was the foundation of DSM (Diamond Star Motors) drag racing culture. Billet 4G63 blocks with large-frame turbos reached 1,500+ horsepower in purpose-built drag cars, proving four cylinders could match V8 power.

The Honda K-series engine (K20/K24) replaced the B-series as the dominant Honda drag racing platform in the 2010s. Turbo K-series builds regularly run 7-second quarter miles in lightweight Civic and Integra chassis. The K24's 2.4L displacement provides a strong foundation for forced induction.

The Nissan R35 GT-R, launched in 2007, became a drag racing monster thanks to its twin-turbo VR38DETT V6 and sophisticated AWD launch control. AMS Performance's Alpha 16 GT-R package ran a 7.7-second quarter mile, proving the R35 could compete with dedicated drag cars.

Big block Chevy (BBC) engines — particularly the 454, 496, 540, and 632 cubic inch configurations — remain the backbone of sportsman and bracket drag racing. The 632 CI BBC, built from aftermarket blocks, produces 800+ naturally aspirated horsepower and is the displacement king of amateur drag racing.

Roots-type superchargers in drag racing are sized by their displacement per revolution: 6-71, 8-71, 10-71, 12-71, and 14-71 (named after Detroit Diesel engine applications). A 14-71 blower on a 500 CI Hemi at 50 PSI boost produces approximately 8,000-11,000 horsepower on nitromethane.

ProCharger centrifugal superchargers dominate the radial-tire racing classes. Unlike positive-displacement Roots blowers, centrifugal superchargers increase boost with RPM, producing a progressive power curve. F-1X and F-3R-136 models support 2,000-3,500+ horsepower in Pro Mod and RVW applications.

Holley Dominator and Sniper EFI systems replaced carburetors in many drag racing classes during the 2010s. Electronic fuel injection provides precise fuel delivery across all RPM ranges, automatic compensation for air density changes, and data logging — eliminating the carburetor jet-changing ritual between rounds.

Hilborn mechanical fuel injection, introduced in the 1950s, uses a constant-flow injection system driven off the engine. It's still used in nostalgia drag racing classes. The characteristic eight-stack injector setup on an exposed engine is one of the most iconic images in drag racing.

MSD (Multiple Spark Discharge) ignition systems have been standard in drag racing since the 1970s. The MSD Digital 7 and Power Grid systems fire multiple sparks per ignition event at low RPM and provide precise timing control via programmable curves. Crank trigger setups replaced distributors for more accurate timing in high-RPM applications.

Mickey Thompson ET Street and ET Drag radial tires are the most popular street-legal drag tires. The ET Street R is DOT-approved for street use but features a drag-compound tread. Mickey Thompson also makes dedicated slicks — the Pro Bracket Radial is the standard in bracket racing.

Tire shake in drag racing occurs when the rear tires lose and regain traction in rapid cycles, creating violent vibration that can damage the drivetrain and chassis. Causes include too much power for available traction, incorrect tire pressure, wrong shock valving, or track surface issues. Experienced drivers pedal (momentarily lift the throttle) to save the run.

Chromoly 4130 steel tubing is the standard material for drag racing roll cages and chassis. NHRA SFI specifications dictate minimum tube diameter and wall thickness based on ET and speed. A typical sportsman chassis uses 1.625" x 0.083" main hoops and 1.375" x 0.065" secondary bars.

RacePak and MoTeC data acquisition systems record engine RPM, boost, EGT (exhaust gas temperature), oil pressure, fuel pressure, driveshaft speed, and accelerometer data at 100+ samples per second. Crew chiefs analyze this data between rounds to diagnose problems and optimize performance.

Delay boxes are electronic devices used in bracket racing that allow the driver to set a programmed delay between the green light and the activation of the transbrake release. The driver reacts to the tree, but the box controls the actual launch timing. Some classes allow them, others (no-box) prohibit them.

The Lenco four-speed transmission is a clutchless, air-shifted planetary gearbox used in Top Fuel, Funny Car, and high-end Pro Mod cars. Shifts occur in approximately 0.04 seconds via CO2 air shifters. The transmission uses individual planetary gearsets that can be changed between rounds for different track conditions.

Larry Larson's 1966 Chevy II (later his S10 truck) was one of the most famous grudge and no-prep cars, running deep 6-second quarter miles with a twin-turbo big block Chevy while being street-driven to events. His Drag Week exploits proved a truly fast car could be street-legal.

The 'Sick Seconds' twin-turbo 1969 Camaro, owned by Farah and Feras Khouri, became famous as one of the fastest street-driven cars in the world, running 6-second quarter miles and then driving home. It represents the pinnacle of street/strip car building.

Connie Kalitta, known as 'The Bounty Hunter,' was one of the original Top Fuel legends of the 1960s-70s. He later became a successful airline cargo operator (Kalitta Air) and continued as a team owner in NHRA for decades. His son Scott Kalitta's death in 2008 was one of the sport's greatest tragedies.

The Ford Coyote 5.0L V8 (Gen 1-3, 2011-present) emerged as a drag racing platform in the 2010s. With DOHC, VVT, and an aluminum block, it responds well to forced induction. Twin-turbo Coyote S550 Mustangs run 7-second quarter miles, challenging the LS's drag racing dominance.

E85 ethanol fuel (85% ethanol, 15% gasoline) became popular in drag racing because it has a higher octane rating than pump gasoline (105 vs 93), burns cooler, and is cheap. Engines can run more ignition timing and boost on E85 than gasoline, gaining 5-15% power depending on the combination.

Wide-band oxygen sensors (Lambda sensors) revolutionized drag racing tuning by providing real-time air/fuel ratio data. Tuners target specific AFR values for each stage of a run — richer for safety at launch (11:1), leaner for power at top end (12.5:1). Innovate, AEM, and Bosch are the dominant brands.

Progressive nitrous controllers (like those from NOS, Nitrous Express, and Edelbrock) ramp nitrous delivery from a low percentage at launch to full power as the car gains traction. This prevents tire spin at launch while still delivering maximum power at the top end. Controllers can be programmed based on RPM, time, or speed.

The Texas Mile, held at the Chase Field Industrial Complex in Beeville, Texas, is a standing-mile top speed event where drag-style builds test their top-end speed over a full mile. Several cars have exceeded 250 mph, bridging the gap between drag racing and land speed racing.

Bonneville Salt Flats Speed Week, organized by the Southern California Timing Association (SCTA), attracts many drag racers who also pursue land speed records. The flat, natural surface of the salt flats near Wendover, Utah, has been used for speed record attempts since 1914.

The Gatornationals, held annually at Gainesville Raceway in Gainesville, Florida, is one of the most prestigious events on the NHRA calendar. First held in 1970, it traditionally marks the opening of the eastern swing and has been the site of numerous records and championship moments.

The U.S. Nationals, held at Lucas Oil Indianapolis Raceway Park (formerly Indianapolis Raceway Park) in Clermont, Indiana, is the biggest and most prestigious event in NHRA drag racing. First held in 1955, it's the sport's equivalent of the Super Bowl. Winning the U.S. Nationals is every drag racer's dream.

The March Meet at Famoso Raceway in McFarland, California, is the premier nostalgia drag racing event, featuring front-engine Top Fuel dragsters, vintage Funny Cars, and cackle cars (push-started nitro cars that idle on nitromethane — the distinctive 'cackle' sound). It's a living museum of drag racing history.

Cummins diesel drag racing emerged in the 2000s with heavily modified Dodge Ram trucks running compound turbo setups producing 2,000+ horsepower. The Scheid Diesel trucks ran 7-second quarter miles, proving diesel engines could compete with gasoline-powered drag cars.

The 13B rotary engine from the Mazda RX-7 became a drag racing cult favorite despite its unusual design. Turbo rotary builds with peripheral-ported housings and massive single turbos have run 6-second quarter miles. The engine's compact size and high-RPM power delivery suit lightweight drag builds.
